criteria: The agent has read Section 1 (Introduction) completely to understand the motivation and the three core contributions: the DSG-ViT block, the MTA module, and the overall DSGA-Net architecture.
The agent has read Section 2.3 to understand the baseline Separable Vision Transformer (SepViT) mechanism (DWA and PWA, Equations 1-2) that DSG-ViT extends.
The agent has read Section 3.1 and Figure 2 completely to understand the Deep Separable Gated Visual Transformer (DSG-ViT) module and its internal DSG-Attn mechanism composed of DWGA and PWGA.
The agent has read Section 3.2 and Figure 3 completely to understand the Mixed Three-branch Attention (MTA) module combining channel, spatial, and global self-attention.
The agent has read Section 3.3 and Figures 4-5 to understand the overall DSGA-Net network structure: the 4-layer DSG Block Encoder, MTA-based skip connections, and the cascaded Decoder.
The agent has read Section 4.1 to understand the three experimental datasets (Synapse, ACDC, BraTs2020), including their sizes, splits, and label categories.
The agent has read Section 4.2 to understand the experiment setup, training hyperparameters, and evaluation metrics (DSC, HD).
The agent has read Section 4.3 completely to understand the four ablation studies: DSG-ViT/MTA contribution, number of skip connections, input resolution, and number of DSG-ViT modules.
The agent has read Section 4.4 and Tables 5-8 to understand the comparisons with state-of-the-art methods on the Synapse, ACDC, and BraTs2020 datasets.
The agent has read the Conclusion (Section 5) to understand the noted limitation of high GPU memory/computational cost introduced by the global self-attention mechanism.
The plan includes replacing SepViT's DWA and PWA with a gated, position-aware DSG-Attn mechanism composed of Depthwise Gate Self-Attention (DWGA) and Pointwise Gate Self-Attention (PWGA).
The plan includes four learnable gated position embeddings (G_Q, G_K, G_V1, G_V2) that weight relative position bias terms for query, key, and value in DWGA.
The plan includes the window-token-based cross-window attention (PWGA), including the LayerNorm and GELU activation applied to window tokens before computing the cross-window attention map.
The plan includes wrapping DSG-Attn with a residual connection followed by a LayerNorm and MLP block to form a complete DSG-ViT block.
The plan includes a 4-stage Encoder where each stage is a DSG Block built from multiple stacked DSG-ViT blocks, with patch-merging downsampling between stages.

The code implements Depthwise Gate Self-Attention (DWGA) exactly as Equation 3: a local m x m neighborhood softmax over the content term q^T k plus gated relative position bias terms (G_Q, G_K), combined with a gated value term (G_V1 v_p + G_V2 r^v).
The code implements the query, key, and value linear projections (W_Q, W_K, W_V) used by both DWGA and PWGA, as defined in Equation 4.

The code implements Pointwise Gate Self-Attention (PWGA) exactly as Equation 5: cross-window attention using window tokens with gated relative position encoding (G_Q, G_K) applied to the content term q^T k.
The code implements extraction of window tokens from the DWGA output and applies LayerNorm followed by GELU activation prior to computing the PWGA attention map.
The code implements a complete DSG-ViT block combining DSG-Attn (DWGA + PWGA) with LayerNorm, MLP, and residual connections as shown in Figure 2.
The code implements patch-merging downsampling layers between the 4 DSG Block stages of the Encoder.
The code implements the 4-stage Encoder with DSG-ViT block counts of (2, 2, 6, 2) per stage, matching the final 'Middle-1' configuration selected in the paper.
The plan includes the channel attention branch of MTA: max pooling and average pooling on the input feature map, each followed by a 1x1 convolution, LeakyReLU, elementwise addition, and Sigmoid.
The plan includes the multi-scale spatial attention branch of MTA using three parallel convolution branches with kernel sizes 7x7, 5x5, and 3x3.

The plan includes the global self-attention branch of MTA based on a ViT-style design: patch flattening, learnable position embedding, multi-head self-attention, LayerNorm+MLP, and reconstruction back into a feature map.

The plan includes combining the channel attention map and spatial attention map via elementwise multiplication, then adding the global self-attention map to form the final MTA output, per Equation 15.

The plan includes applying an MTA module at the skip connections from DSG Block-1, DSG Block-2, and DSG Block-3, while DSG Block-4's output uses a plain 3x3 convolution instead of MTA due to its small spatial size.

The code implements the channel attention map C(X) exactly per Equations 6-8: MaxPool and AveragePool branches each passed through a convolution and LeakyReLU, summed elementwise, and passed through Sigmoid.
The code implements the multi-scale spatial attention feature S_p for kernel sizes k={3,5,7} with corresponding padding p={1,2,3}, each followed by Batch Normalization and ReLU, per Equation 9.
The code implements the summation of the three multi-scale branches T = sum(S_p) and the Sigmoid activation producing the spatial attention map S, per Equations 10-11.

The code implements the global self-attention branch: patch flattening with a learnable position code c_loc, multi-head self-attention (MHSA), LayerNorm+MLP with residual connections, and the SeqImg operation reconstructing the global attention map G, per Equations 12-14.
The code implements the final MTA combination MTA = (C ⊗ S) ⊕ G exactly as Equation 15.
The code feeds each DSG Block's output feature map into its corresponding MTA module before fusing the result with the Decoder layer at the matching resolution.
The plan includes the overall encoder-decoder architecture: the 4-layer DSG Block Encoder, MTA-based skip connections at three of the four layers, and a cascaded upsampling Decoder.

The plan includes the Decoder design where each layer performs 2x2 upsampling, 3x3 convolution, and ReLU activation, with a final 1x1 convolution mapping channels to the number of segmentation classes.

The code implements the Decoder upsampling path with feature concatenation of the MTA outputs at multiple resolutions, matching the channel/resolution flow shown in Figure 4 (e.g., 96 channels at H/4 from each of the four branches before concatenation).
The code implements the final 1x1 convolution segmentation head that maps the concatenated decoder features to per-class segmentation logits.
The code implements the full DSGA-Net forward pass integrating the Encoder, the per-layer MTA modules, and the Decoder end-to-end into a single trainable model.

The plan includes loading and preprocessing the Synapse Multi-Organ dataset (30 abdominal CT scans, 3779 axial slices) with an 18/12 case train/validation split (2212 training slices) and 8 organ labels (aorta, gallbladder, left/right kidney, liver, pancreas, spleen, stomach).
The plan includes loading and preprocessing the ACDC dataset with a 70 training (1930 slices)/10 validation/20 test split and LV/RV/Myocardium labels, evaluated using mean DSC only.

The plan includes loading and preprocessing the BraTs2020 dataset (369 training cases, 125 validation cases) with four MRI modalities (Flair, T1, T1ce, T2) and four label categories (background, NCR/NET, ED, ET).
The code implements loading and slicing of the Synapse CT volumes into the specified 18/12 case train/validation split for the 8 organ classes.
The code implements loading and slicing of the ACDC MRI volumes into the specified 70/10/20 train/validation/test split, computing the mean DSC over LV/RV/Myo.
The code implements loading of the BraTs2020 dataset with its four MRI modalities and computes the WT (NCR/NET+ED+ET) and TC (NCR/NET+ET) evaluation regions exactly as defined in Equation 16.
The code implements data augmentation (random flips and rotations) applied to training samples for all three datasets.
The plan notes the hardware/software environment used in the original experiments (Windows 10, Nvidia RTX 2070s GPU, Python 3.8, and the deep learning framework version 1.10.1).
The plan includes the training configuration: 224x224 input resolution, SGD optimizer with learning rate 0.01, momentum 0.9, weight decay 0.0004, and batch size 24.
The code implements the SGD optimizer with learning rate 0.01, momentum 0.9, and weight decay 0.0004 as specified.
The code sets the input resolution to 224x224 and batch size to 24 for the main experiments.
The code implements the evaluation metrics: average Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) and average Hausdorff Distance (HD), used consistently across all experiments.
The training pipeline executes end-to-end without errors on at least one dataset (e.g., Synapse), producing DSC and HD metrics.
The plan includes the four-group ablation: (1) TransUNet baseline, (2) baseline+DSG-ViT, (3) baseline+MTA, (4) baseline+DSG-ViT+MTA (DSGA-Net), as defined in Table 1.
The code implements a configurable TransUNet baseline model usable as ablation Group 1.
The code implements model variants with only the DSG-ViT module added (Group 2) and only the MTA module added (Group 3) on top of the TransUNet baseline.
All four ablation groups (Baseline, +DSG-ViT, +MTA, +DSG-ViT+MTA) have been trained and evaluated on the Synapse dataset.

The execution outcomes match Table 2: DSGA-Net (Group 4) achieves the best average DSC (~81.24%) and lowest HD (~20.91%), improving substantially over the TransUNet baseline (Group 1, DSC 77.48%, HD 31.69%).

The execution outcomes confirm that Groups 2 and 3 each individually improve over the baseline (DSC +1.06% for DSG-ViT alone, +1.27% for MTA alone), consistent with the additive ablation trend in Table 2.

The execution outcomes' qualitative segmentation visualizations show that removing MTA causes over-segmentation errors (e.g., spleen confused with liver, left/right kidney confusion) and removing DSG-ViT causes inaccurate organ-edge prediction, consistent with Figure 6.
The plan includes testing 0-MTA, 1-MTA, 2-MTA, and 3-MTA skip-connection configurations, progressively adding MTA modules between DSG Block-1/2/3 and the Decoder.
The code implements a configurable number of MTA skip connections (0 to 3) between Encoder and Decoder stages for the ablation study.
The 0-MTA, 1-MTA, 2-MTA, and 3-MTA configurations have been trained and evaluated on the Synapse dataset, reporting average DSC across the 8 organs.

The execution outcomes match Figure 7: the 3-MTA configuration achieves the best average DSC, with larger relative improvement for small organs (aorta, gallbladder, kidney, pancreas) than for large organs (liver, stomach).
The plan includes comparing 224x224 vs 512x512 input resolution for both DSGA-Net and TransUNet on the Synapse dataset.
The code supports configurable input resolution (224 or 512) for both training and evaluation.
Both DSGA-Net and TransUNet have been trained and evaluated at 224x224 and 512x512 resolutions on the Synapse dataset.

The execution outcomes match Table 3: increasing resolution to 512x512 improves average DSC for both models, but DSGA-Net shows a smaller relative gain than TransUNet, indicating greater robustness to resolution change.
The plan includes testing three Encoder configurations with DSG-ViT block counts of (1,1,3,1) ('Base'), (2,2,6,2) ('Middle-1'), and (3,3,9,3) ('Middle-2').
The code implements a configurable number of DSG-ViT blocks per Encoder stage to support the Base/Middle-1/Middle-2 ablation.
The Base, Middle-1, and Middle-2 Encoder configurations have been trained and evaluated on the Synapse dataset.

The execution outcomes match Table 4: average DSC increases from Base (78.16%) to Middle-1 (81.24%) to Middle-2 (82.23%), with diminishing returns from Middle-1 to Middle-2, justifying the choice of Middle-1 (2,2,6,2) as the final model.
The plan includes implementing and training the CNN-based baselines (U-Net, Atten-UNet, R50-UNet, R50-Atten-UNet) for comparison on the Synapse dataset.
The plan includes implementing and training the CNN+ViT baselines (ViT, SepViT, R50-ViT, TransUNet, SwinUNet) for comparison on the Synapse dataset.
The plan includes training each comparison experiment on Synapse three times and averaging the results for reliability, as stated in Section 4.4.1.
The code implements or integrates the CNN-based baseline models (U-Net, Atten-UNet, R50-UNet, R50-Atten-UNet) used in Table 5.
The code implements or integrates the CNN+ViT baseline models (ViT, SepViT, R50-ViT, TransUNet, SwinUNet) used in Table 6.
DSGA-Net and all comparison baselines have been trained and evaluated on the Synapse dataset, each averaged over three runs.
The execution outcomes match Table 5: DSGA-Net achieves the best average DSC (81.24%), the lowest HD (20.91%), and the lowest FLOPs among the CNN-based models compared.

The execution outcomes match Table 6: DSGA-Net outperforms all CNN+ViT hybrid baselines in average DSC and HD on Synapse, with particularly large gains on small organs such as the gallbladder and pancreas.

The execution outcomes confirm that DSGA-Net's computational complexity (FLOPs) in Table 6 is the lowest among compared methods but not substantially lower than TransUNet, consistent with the paper's discussion of remaining computational burden.
The plan includes implementing and training the comparison baselines (R50-UNet, R50-Atten-UNet, R50-ViT, SwinUNet, TransUNet) on the ACDC dataset.
DSGA-Net and all comparison baselines have been trained and evaluated on the ACDC dataset.
The execution outcomes match Table 7: DSGA-Net achieves the best average DSC (91.34%) on ACDC, with particularly strong gains on the myocardium (Myo) and left ventricle (LV) classes.
The plan includes implementing and training the comparison baselines (U-Net, R50-UNet, R50-Atten-UNet, SwinUNet, TransUNet) on the BraTs2020 dataset.
DSGA-Net and all comparison baselines have been trained and evaluated on the BraTs2020 dataset using the WT/TC/ET region metrics.
The execution outcomes match Table 8: DSGA-Net achieves the best average DSC (85.82%) and lowest HD (5.27%) on BraTs2020, with notable gains on the enhanced tumor (ET) region.
